name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنَآ ۗ أَفَمَن يُلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلنَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَم مَّن يَأْتِىٓ ءَامِنًا يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوا۟ مَا شِئْتُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
Those who abuse Our signs are not hidden from Us. Is someone who is cast in the Fire better off, or someone who arrives safely on the Day of Resurrection? Act as you wish; indeed He watches what you do. (40)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَكِتَـٰبٌ عَزِيزٌ (٤١)
```

```english
Those who reject the Reminder when it comes to them... Indeed, it is an august Book: (41)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَأْتِيهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلَا مِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ ۖ تَنزِيلٌ مِّنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ (٤٢)
```

```english
falsehood cannot approach it, at present or in future, a [revelation gradually] sent down from One all-wise, all-laudable. (42)
```

```arabic
مَّا يُقَالُ لَكَ إِلَّا مَا قَدْ قِيلَ لِلرُّسُلِ مِن قَبْلِكَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ وَذُو عِقَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (٤٣)
```

```english
Nothing is said to you except what has already been said to the apostles before you. Indeed your Lord is One who forgives and also metes out a painful retribution. (43)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَانًا أَعْجَمِيًّا لَّقَالُوا۟ لَوْلَا فُصِّلَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥٓ ۖ ءَا۬عْجَمِىٌّ وَعَرَبِىٌّ ۗ قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ هُدًى وَشِفَآءٌ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ فِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرٌ وَهُوَ عَلَيْهِمْ عَمًى ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يُنَادَوْنَ مِن مَّكَانٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٤٤)
```

```english
Had We made it a non-Arabic Quran, they would have said, ‘Why have not its signs been articulated?’ ‘What! A non-Arabian [scripture] and an Arabian [prophet]!?’ Say, ‘It is guidance and healing for those who have faith. As for those who are faithless, there is a deafness in their ears and it is lost to their sight: [to them it is as if] they were called from a distant place.’ (44)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَى ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ فَٱخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ ۗ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (٤٥)
```

```english
Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose about it; and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord, judgement would have been made between them, for they are in grave doubt concerning it. (45)
```

```arabic
مَّنْ عَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَنْ أَسَآءَ فَعَلَيْهَا ۗ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّـٰمٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (٤٦)
```

```english
Whoever acts righteously, it is for [the benefit of] his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its detriment, and your Lord is not tyrannical to His servants. (46)
```

```arabic
۞ إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۚ وَمَا تَخْرُجُ مِن ثَمَرَٰتٍ مِّنْ أَكْمَامِهَا وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۚ وَيَوْمَ يُنَادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِى قَالُوٓا۟ ءَاذَنَّـٰكَ مَا مِنَّا مِن شَهِيدٍ (٤٧)
```

```english
On Him devolves the knowledge of the Hour, and no fruit emerges from its covering and no female conceives or delivers except with His knowledge. On the day when He will call out to them, ‘Where are My ‘partners’?’ They will say, ‘We informed You that there is no witness amongst us.’ (47)
```

```arabic
وَضَلَّ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَدْعُونَ مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَظَنُّوا۟ مَا لَهُم مِّن مَّحِيصٍ (٤٨)
```

```english
What they used to invoke before has forsaken them, and they know there is no escape for them. (48)
```

```arabic
لَّا يَسْـَٔمُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِن دُعَآءِ ٱلْخَيْرِ وَإِن مَّسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَيَـُٔوسٌ قَنُوطٌ (٤٩)
```

```english
Man is never wearied of supplicating for good, and should any ill befall him, he becomes hopeless and despondent. (49)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ ضَرَّآءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هَـٰذَا لِى وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّجِعْتُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ إِنَّ لِى عِندَهُۥ لَلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ فَلَنُنَبِّئَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُم مِّنْ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (٥٠)
```

```english
If, after distress has befallen him, We let him have a taste of Our mercy, he says, ‘This is my due! I do not think the Hour will ever come, and in case I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best [reward] with Him.’ So We will surely inform the faithless about what they have done, and will surely make them taste a harsh punishment. (50)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَآ أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى ٱلْإِنسَـٰنِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَـَٔا بِجَانِبِهِۦ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ ٱلشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَآءٍ عَرِيضٍ (٥١)
```

```english
When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when an ill befalls him, he makes protracted supplications. (51)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ إِن كَانَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ثُمَّ كَفَرْتُم بِهِۦ مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ هُوَ فِى شِقَاقٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٥٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, who will be more astray than those who are in extreme defiance.’ (52)
```

```arabic
سَنُرِيهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فِى ٱلْـَٔافَاقِ وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ حَتَّىٰ يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (٥٣)
```

```english
Soon We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and in their own souls until it becomes clear to them that He is the Real. Is it not sufficient that your Lord is witness to all things? (53)
```

```arabic
أَلَآ إِنَّهُمْ فِى مِرْيَةٍ مِّن لِّقَآءِ رَبِّهِمْ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّحِيطٌۢ (٥٤)
```

```english
Indeed, they are in doubt about the encounter with their Lord! Indeed, He embraces all things! (54)
```

## (Commentary)

This is another return to the discourse concerning the Quran: their disbelief in it despite the manifestation of its signs and the loftiness of its station; their negligence regarding their duty toward Allah; their slandering of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); their denial of the truth and disbelief in the signs, and what follows from that. With this, the Surah concludes.

The first verse—namely His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي آياتِنا** (Indeed those who profanely distort Our signs...)—is like an intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *a connecting link* — between this section and the preceding section of verses, as it is situated between His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ** (Those who reject the Reminder when it comes to them...) and His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لا تَسْمَعُوا لِهذَا الْقُرْآنِ** (The faithless say, ‘Do not listen to this Quran...’) and His saying: **وَمِنْ آياتِهِ اللَّيْلُ وَالنَّهارُ** (Among His signs are the night and the day...), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُلْحِدُونَ فِي آياتِنا لا يَخْفَوْنَ عَلَيْنا** (Indeed those who profanely distort Our signs are not hidden from Us...) is a context of threat directed at the distorters of this nation, as supported by the subsequent verse. Profane distortion (الإلحاد) means deviation (الميل).

The general nature of His saying: "profanely distort" (يُلْحِدُونَ) and "Our signs" (آياتِنا) encompasses every distortion in every sign. Thus, it includes distortion in the creative signs (الآيات التكوينية) such as the sun, the moon, and others, whereby they acknowledge them as signs of Allah, the Glorified, but then turn to worship them. It also includes the signs of revelation and prophethood, whereby they consider the Quran a fabrication against Allah and a speech invented by the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), or they engage in idle talk (يلغون) during its recitation so that its reading becomes disrupted and no listener hears it, or they interpret it according to their own opinions, or they provide false interpretations (يؤولونه) seeking sedition. All of this constitutes profane distortion (إلحاد) in the signs of Allah by placing them out of their proper context and deviating them from their true station.

His saying: **أَفَمَنْ يُلْقى فِي النَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَمْ مَنْ يَأْتِي آمِناً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** (Is he who is cast into the Fire better, or he who comes safe on the Day of Resurrection?) is an announcement of the recompense, which is being cast into the Fire on the Day of Resurrection by force, without any expected security such as an intercessor, a helper, or an acceptable excuse; they have nothing but the Fire into which they are cast. It appears that His saying: "or he who comes safe on the Day of Resurrection" is to clarify that there are two groups with no third: those who are upright in faith regarding the signs and those who distort them. It further clarifies that the people of uprightness (أهل الاستقامة) shall be in security on the Day of Resurrection.

His saying: **اعْمَلُوا ما شِئْتُمْ إِنَّهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ** (Do whatever you take a fancy to! Indeed He watches what you do) is an intensification of the threat.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ** (Those who reject the Reminder when it comes to them...) until His saying: **مِنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ** (...from One All-wise, All-laudable). What is meant by the Reminder (الذكر) is the Quran, because of the remembrance of Allah it contains. Restricting the sentence with His saying: "when it comes to them" (لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ) indicates that those meant by "those who reject" are the Arab polytheists contemporary to the Quran, from Quraysh and others.

The grammarians have differed regarding the predicate (خبر) of "Indeed" (إِنَّ). It can be inferred from the context that it is omitted, indicated by His saying: "Indeed those who profanely distort Our signs...", for disbelief in the Quran is among the instances of profane distortion in the signs of Allah. Thus, the implied meaning is: "Indeed those who reject the Reminder when it comes to them shall be cast into the Fire on the Day of Resurrection." It was omitted so that the listener's imagination may go to every possible extent of dread, as the speech is intended for warning (الوعيد).

To this meaning returns the view of al-Zamakhshari in *al-Kashshaf*: that His saying: "Indeed those who reject..." is a substitute (بدل) for His saying: "Indeed those who profanely distort Our signs."

It has also been said that the predicate of "Indeed" is the upcoming verse: **أُولئِكَ يُنادَوْنَ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ** (They are being called from a distant place). It was also said the predicate is His saying: **لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** (falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it), with the omission of a pronoun referring back to the subject of "Indeed." The implied meaning would be: "falsehood does not come to it from them"—meaning that nothing that could invalidate it comes from their direction, nor are they capable of that—or by making the definite article in "the falsehood" (الباطل) a substitute for the pronoun, meaning "their falsehood does not come to it."

It was also said that His saying: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ** (and indeed it is an august Book) stands in the place of the predicate, the meaning being: "Indeed those who reject the Reminder have rejected it, and indeed it is an august Book."

It was also said the predicate is His saying: **ما يُقالُ لَكَ** (Nothing is said to you...) with the omission of the pronoun "concerning them" (فيهم). The meaning would be: "Nothing is said to you concerning those who reject the Reminder except what has already been said to the apostles before you: that for them is the punishment of extirpation in this world and the punishment of the Fire in the Hereafter." The elements of affectation (التكلف) in these views are not hidden from the insightful observer.

In His saying: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ** (and indeed it is an august Book), the pronoun refers to the Reminder, which is the Quran. The "august" (العزيز) is that which has no peer, or that which is invulnerable (المنيع), impossible to be overcome. The second meaning is more appropriate given what follows it: **لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** (falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it).

Regarding His saying: **لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** (falsehood cannot approach it from before it nor from behind it), the "approach of falsehood" to it means its entry into it, such that some or all of its parts become false—by its true knowledge (المعارف الحقة) or some of it becoming untrue, or its laws (الأحكام), legislations (الشرائع), and the ethics (الأخلاق) attached to them becoming vain (لغوا) and unworthy of practice.

Accordingly, what is meant by "from before it nor from behind it" (مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ) temporally is the present and the future—meaning the time of revelation and what follows it until the Day of Resurrection. It has also been said that "from before it and from behind it" refers to all directions, like morning and evening, as a metaphor for all time; thus, it is protected from falsehood from all sides. This generality, according to the first view, is inferred from the absolute negation in His saying: "cannot approach it" (لا يَأْتِيهِ).

The meaning in any case is that there is no contradiction in its statements, no lie in its reports, and no falsehood can find a way into its knowledge, wisdom, and laws. It cannot be opposed, nor can it be changed by introducing into it what is not of it, or by distorting a verse from one sense to another.

Thus, the verse follows the course of His saying: **إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَزَّلْنَا الذِّكْرَ وَإِنَّا لَهُ لَحافِظُونَ** — **Indeed We have sent down the Reminder, and indeed We will preserve it.** (15:9).

His saying: **تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ** (a revelation from One All-wise, All-laudable) is in the position of a justification for its being an august Book that falsehood cannot approach, etc. That is: how could it not be so when it is a revelation from One who is All-wise (حكيم) — *perfectly precise in His actions* — whose acts are untainted by weakness, and who is All-laudable (حميد) — *absolutely praised*?

His saying, the Exalted: **ما يُقالُ لَكَ إِلَّا ما قَدْ قِيلَ لِلرُّسُلِ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ** (Nothing is said to you except what has already been said to the apostles before you...) The "Nothing" (ما) in "Nothing is said to you" is negatory. The speakers are those who disbelieved, when they said: "He is a sorcerer, or possessed, or a poet talking nonsense, or he wants to rule over us." The speakers of what was said to the apostles were their respective nations.

The meaning is: Nothing is said to you by the disbelievers of your people to whom you were sent and whom you invited—whereby they slandered you with what they slandered—except what had already been said to the apostles before you; that is, the likes of what was said to them.

His saying: **إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو مَغْفِرَةٍ وَذُو عِقابٍ أَلِيمٍ** (Indeed your Lord is a possessor of forgiveness and a possessor of a painful punishment) is in the position of threat and warning. That is: Indeed your Lord possesses these two attributes; so look—or let them look—at what will strike them from their Lord while they say what they say to His Messenger. Is it forgiveness or punishment? Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: "Do whatever you take a fancy to! Indeed He watches what you do"—meaning whatever good or evil you do, its exact recompense will reach you.

It has also been said the meaning is: Nothing is revealed to you concerning the matter of these who reject the Reminder except what was revealed to the apostles before you, which is that your Lord is a possessor of forgiveness and a possessor of a painful punishment. Thus, "saying" (القول) refers to revelation (الوحي), and "Indeed your Lord..." is an explanation of what was said.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً أَعْجَمِيًّا لَقالُوا لَوْ لا فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُءَ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَعَرَبِيٌ** (Had We made it a non-Arabic Quran, they would have said, ‘Why have its signs not been articulated? What! A non-Arabic [Book] and an Arabic [audience]?’) Al-Raghib said: "Non-Arabic nature (العجمة) is the opposite of clarity (الإبانة)." He said: "The non-Arabs (العجم) are the opposite of the Arabs, and the 'Ajami (العجمي) is one attributed to them. The A'jam (الأعجم) is one in whose tongue there is a lack of clarity, whether he be an Arab or a non-Arab, based on the lack of understanding from the non-Arabs." [End of quote]. Thus, the *A'jami* is the non-eloquent Arabic, whether he is from a non-Arabic speaking people or from them but lacks clarity due to a lisp or accent in his tongue. Applying "non-Arabic" (الأعجمي) to speech, like applying "Arabic" (العربي), is metaphorical.

The meaning is: Had We made the Quran non-Arabic—not clarifying its objectives and not eloquent in its composition—those who disbelieve from your people would have said: "Why were its signs and parts not articulated and clarified, such that they are distinguished from one another through Arabic and eloquence? Is it a non-Arabic sent Book and an Arabic recipient?" Meaning, they are incompatible and disproportionate.

He said: "Arabic" (عَرَبِيٌ) and did not say "Arabs" or "Arabic people," despite the recipients being a group—the community of Arabs—because the intent is the mere quality of being Arabic without regard for plurality. Rather, the intent is to show the incompatibility between the speech and the one addressed by it, not to specify whether the addressee is one or many.

Al-Zamakhshari said in *al-Kashshaf*: "If you ask: How can 'Arabic' refer to the recipients when they are the nation of Arabs? I say: it is according to what must occur in the denunciation of something objectionable; if one saw a non-Arabic book written to a group of Arabs, he would say: 'A non-Arabic book and an Arabic recipient.' This is because the basis of the denunciation is the discordance between the state of the book and the state of the recipient, not whether the recipient is one or a group. Thus, it was necessary to strip it down to the purpose for which it was driven and not attach to it what would disrupt another purpose. Do you not see that when you see a long garment on a short woman, you say: 'The garment is long and the wearer is short'? If you said 'the wearer is a short woman,' you would have brought something that is a linguistic stammer and superfluous speech, because the discourse did not concern the masculinity or femininity of the wearer; it concerned a purpose beyond them."

His saying: **قُلْ هُوَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا هُدىً وَشِفاءٌ** (Say, ‘It is guidance and a healing for those who have faith...’) is a clarification that the effect of the Quran and its special property do not revolve around its language. Rather, people toward it are of two types: those who believe and those who do not believe. It is guidance (هدى) and healing (شفاء) for those who believe—guiding them to the truth and healing the sickness of doubt and suspicion in their hearts. And it is blindness for those who do not believe—those in whose ears is a heaviness—it blinds them so they do not see the truth or the path of rectitude.

In describing those who do not believe as having "heaviness in their ears," there is an allusion to their own admission of this, as narrated from them at the beginning of the Surah: **وَفِي آذانِنا وَقْرٌ** (...and in our ears is a heaviness).

His saying: **أُولئِكَ يُنادَوْنَ مِنْ مَكانٍ بَعِيدٍ** (They are being called from a distant place) means they neither hear the voice nor see the person. This is a parable for their state, as they do not accept admonition nor comprehend proof.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا مُوسَى الْكِتابَ فَاخْتُلِفَ فِيهِ** (Certainly We gave Moses the Book, but differences arose concerning it...) is a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding his people's denial and disbelief in his Book.

His saying: **وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ** (Had it not been for a prior decree from your Lord, the matter would have been decided between them). The "decree" (الكلمة) is His saying: **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ** — **‘There will be for you an abode on the earth and a provision for a specified time.’** (7:24).

His saying: **وَإِنَّهُمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ** (and they are indeed in a skeptical doubt about it) means in a disquieting doubt concerning the Book of Moses (عليه‌السلام). This is a clarification of the state of his people so that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) may be consoled regarding what he sees from his own people.

His saying, the Exalted: **مَنْ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ أَساءَ فَعَلَيْها** (Whoever acts righteously, it is for his own soul, and whoever does evil, it is to its own detriment...) That is, the action is tied to its doer and characterizes him. If it is righteous and beneficial, his soul benefits from it; if it is evil and harmful, his soul is harmed by it. Thus, there is no injustice (ظلم) or misplacement of things in the Exalted's delivering the benefit of a righteous deed to its doer—which is the reward—nor in delivering the harm of an evil deed to its doer—which is the punishment.

If that were injustice, the Exalted would be—in His rewarding and punishing of countless servants for countless deeds—a "great wronger" (ظلام) of the servants. But He is not unjust, nor is the Exalted a "great wronger" of His servants. From this, the reason for using the intensive form (اسم المبالغة) in His saying: **وَما رَبُّكَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِلْعَبِيدِ** (and your Lord is not a wronger of [His] servants) becomes clear, rather than saying "not a wrongdoer" (بظالم).

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَيْهِ يُرَدُّ عِلْمُ السَّاعَةِ** (To Him is referred the knowledge of the Hour...) until His saying: **إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ** (...except with His knowledge). The referral of the knowledge of the Hour to Him means its exclusivity to Him; none knows it but He. This has been repeated in His speech, the Exalted.

His saying: **وَما تَخْرُجُ مِنْ ثَمَراتٍ مِنْ أَكْمامِها** (No fruits emerge from their skins...). "Fruits" (ثَمَراتٍ) is the subject of "emerge" (تَخْرُجُ), and the "from" (مِنْ) is extra for emphasis, like His saying: **وَكَفى بِاللهِ شَهِيداً** — **Allah suffices as a witness.** (4:79). The "skins" (أكمام) is the plural of *kumm*, which is the envelope or sheath of the fruit. The "No" (ما) is the subject whose predicate is "except with His knowledge" (إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ). The meaning is: no fruits emerge from their sheaths, nor does any female conceive or give birth, except accompanied by His knowledge. That is, He, the Exalted, knows the particulars of the states of everything.

Thus, He, the Exalted—along with being the Creator of things and the Changer of their states—is Knower of them and the particulars of their states, and is Watchful over them. This is the most perfect governance (التدبير); thus, He alone is the Lord. In the verse, there is an indication of His uniqueness, the Exalted, in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية). Therefore, He concluded this opening with His saying: **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِي** (On the day He will call them, ‘Where are My partners?’), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَيَوْمَ يُنادِيهِمْ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِي قالُوا آذَنَّاكَ ما مِنَّا مِنْ شَهِيدٍ** (On the day He will call them, ‘Where are My partners?’ they will say, ‘We inform You that there is no witness among us.’) until His saying: **مِنْ مَحِيصٍ** (...any escape). The adverbial phrase of time (الظرف) is connected to His saying: "they will say" (قالُوا). It has also been said it is an adverb for an omitted subsequent word that was left out to indicate that description falls short of it, as in His saying: **يَوْمَ يَجْمَعُ اللهُ الرُّسُلَ** (The day Allah will gather the apostles...). It was also said it is connected to something omitted like "Remember." Perhaps the first view is more appropriate for the beginning of the verse according to the meaning we mentioned; thus, the verse is driven to negate partners by clarifying that governance (التدبير) belongs to Him, the Exalted, and the polytheists' admission of that on the Day of Resurrection.

"Informing" (الإيذان) is notifying. What is meant by "witness" (الشهادة) is either verbal testimony or witnessing in the sense of presential vision (الرؤية الحضورية). According to the second sense, His saying: **وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَدْعُونَ مِنْ قَبْلُ** (What they used to invoke before will fail them) is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسير) clarifying the reason for the absence of witnessing.

His saying: **وَظَنُّوا ما لَهُمْ مِنْ مَحِيصٍ** (and they will know that they have no escape). "Thought" (الظن)—as has been said—is in the sense of certainty (اليقين). The "escape" (المحيص) is the place of flight or refuge. The meaning is: On the day Allah calls the polytheists: "Where are My partners?"—according to your claim—they will say: "We have informed You that there is none among us who testifies for You having partners"—or none among us who witnesses the partners—and what they used to invoke besides Allah in the world will have vanished from them, and they will be certain that they have no refuge from the punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: **لا يَسْأَمُ الْإِنْسانُ مِنْ دُعاءِ الْخَيْرِ وَإِنْ مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَيَؤُسٌ قَنُوطٌ** (Man does not tire of supplicating for good, but if ill touches him, he is despondent and hopeless). "Tiring" (السأمة) is boredom. "Despondency and hopelessness" (اليأس والقنوط) carry the same meaning, which is the severing of hope. "Supplicating" (الدعاء) is seeking.

This is the beginning of the conclusion of the discourse in the Surah by clarifying the reason for their denial and rejection of the explicit truth. It is that man is deluded by himself; when an evil touches him that he is unable to repel, he despairs of good and clings to the hem of supplication and petition, turning to his Lord. But when good touches him, he becomes occupied with it, admires himself, and this makes him forget every truth and reality.

The meaning is: Man does not tire of seeking good—which is what he sees as beneficial for his life and livelihood—but if evil touches him, he is very despondent and hopeless because of what he sees as the collapse of the means (الأسباب) upon which he used to rely. This does not contradict his hope being attached then to Allah, the Glorified, as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي** (And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after the distress that has touched him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due...’). Originally, considering the content of the previous verse, it should have said: "And if he tastes good, he says: This is mine." However, "tasted" was replaced with "We let him taste" (أَذَقْناهُ) and "good" with "a mercy from Us" (رَحْمَةً مِنَّا) to indicate that the good he tasted is a mercy from Allah that He let him taste, and it is neither a merit of his own nor does he own it. Had he owned it, it would not have left him and distress would not have touched him. Therefore, He restricted His saying: "And if We let him taste..." with His saying: "after the distress that has touched him" (مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ).

His saying: **لَيَقُولَنَّ هذا لِي** (he will surely say, ‘This is my due...’) means: "I own it, so I have the right to do with it as I wish and dispose of it as I want. No one has the right to prevent me from any part of it or hold me accountable for an action." For this reason, He followed it with His saying: **وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً** (and I do not think the Hour will ever set in), for the Hour is the Day of Account.

His saying: **وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى** (and if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best [reward] with Him). That is, for the best reward or the best outcome. This is based on what he sees for himself of dignity and entitlement to good. It is as if he says: "The good I owned, if it were from Allah, it is only because of my dignity before Him. Based on this, if the Hour sets in and I am returned to my Lord, I would have the best outcome with Him."

The meaning is: I swear, if We let man taste a mercy that is from Us—which he neither deserves nor owns—and We let him taste it after distress had touched him, which should have shown him that he does not own what he was given to taste, he forgets what was before and says: "This is mine"—referring to the specific blessing and not calling it a mercy—"and no one has the right to prevent me from what I do with it or hold me accountable for it. I do not think the Hour—which is the Day of Account—will set in. And I swear, if I am returned to my Lord and the Hour sets in, I would have the best outcome with Him due to my dignity before Him, just as He bestowed blessings upon me."

The verse is similar to His saying in the story of the owner of the garden: **ما أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هذِهِ أَبَداً وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلى رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْراً مِنْها مُنْقَلَباً** — **‘I do not think this will ever perish, and I do not think the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord, I will surely find a resort better than this.’** (18:35-36).

Some discussion on this has already preceded.

His saying: **فَلُنُنَبِّئَنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِما عَمِلُوا وَلَنُذِيقَنَّهُمْ مِنْ عَذابٍ غَلِيظٍ** (We will surely inform the faithless of what they have done, and We will surely make them taste a harsh punishment) is a threat and warning.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا أَنْعَمْنا عَلَى الْإِنْسانِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَأى بِجانِبِهِ وَإِذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعاءٍ عَرِيضٍ** (When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns away; but when ill touches him, he is full of prolonged supplications). "Turning away" (النأي) is distancing. What is meant by "side" (الجانب) is the limb, which is the flank, or it means direction and place. Thus, His saying: "he turns away with his side" (نَأى بِجانِبِهِ) is a metaphor for distancing himself, which is a metaphor for arrogance (التكبر) and conceit (الخيلاء). What is meant by "prolonged" (العريض) is wide. A "prolonged supplication" (الدعاء العريض), like a "long supplication," is a metaphor for that which the supplicant continues and persists in. The verse is in the position of censuring and rebuking man: that when Allah blesses him, he turns away from Him and becomes arrogant, and when the blessing is stripped, he remembers Allah and turns to Him with continuous and persistent supplication.

His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ كانَ مِنْ عِنْدِ اللهِ ثُمَّ كَفَرْتُمْ بِهِ مَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ هُوَ فِي شِقاقٍ بَعِيدٍ** (Say, ‘Tell me, if it is from Allah and you disbelieve in it, who is more astray than one who is in a manifest defiance?’). "Tell me" (أَرَأَيْتُمْ) means inform me. "Defiance" (الشقاق والمشاقة) is opposition. "Manifest defiance" (الشقاق البعيد) is opposition that does not approach agreement, and it is severe. His saying: "than one who is in a manifest defiance" is a metaphor for the polytheists. He did not say "than you," but rather used the relative pronoun and its clause, which is in the sense of an attribute, to indicate the cause of the judgment, which is the manifest defiance of the truth.

The meaning is: Say to the polytheists: Inform me, if this Quran is from Allah and then you disbelieve in it, who is more astray than you? That is, none is more astray than you, because you are in an opposition far removed from a truth above which there is no truth.

The import of the verse is that the Quran calls you to Allah, stating that it is from Him. At the very least, there is the possibility of its truthfulness in its claim, and this is sufficient for the obligation of investigating its matter to repel potential harm. And what harm is greater than eternal perdition? Thus, there is no meaning in your turning away from it entirely.

His saying, the Exalted: **سَنُرِيهِمْ آياتِنا فِي الْآفاقِ وَفِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ** (Soon We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and in their own souls until it becomes clear to them that He is the Real...) "Horizons" (الآفاق) is the plural of *ufuq*, which is a region. "Witness" (الشهيد) is in the sense of one who sees (الشاهد) or in the sense of the one witnessed (المشهود), which is appropriate for the context of the verse.

The pronoun in "that it is the truth" (أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ) refers to the Quran, as suggested by the context of the verse and supported by the previous verse mentioning their disbelief in the Quran. Accordingly, the verse promises the showing of signs in the horizons and in their own souls until the truth of the Quran becomes clear through them. The signs whose function is to prove the truth of the Quran are the events and promises that the Quran informed would occur, such as its report that Allah would grant victory to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the believers, establish them in the land, make their religion prevail over all religions, and take vengeance on the polytheists of Quraysh, among other things.

Thus, Allah commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to migrate to Medina when the matter had become severe for him and those who believed in him to the utmost—no sky shaded them and no earth carried them. Then He killed the leaders of Quraysh at Badr, and He continued to raise his mention and grant conquests at his hands until he conquered Mecca and the Arabian Peninsula submitted to him. Then, after his departure, He opened for the Muslims most of the inhabited world. Thus, the Glorified showed the polytheists His signs in the horizons—which are the regions He conquered for the Muslims and in which He spread their religion—and in their own souls, which was their devastating slaughter at Badr.

These are not signs in themselves—for how many conquests and victories does history record, and how many devastating slaughters does it narrate? Rather, they are signs insofar as Allah, the Glorified, promised them and the Noble Quran informed of them before their occurrence, and then they occurred exactly as informed.

It is also possible that what is meant by showing the signs and the clarification of the truth thereby is what is inferred from other verses: that Allah will make His religion prevail in the full sense of prevalence over all religions, such that none but Allah alone is worshipped on earth, and happiness overshadows the human species, which is the goal of their creation. We have previously inferred this from His saying, the Exalted: **وَعَدَ اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنْكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** — **Allah has promised those of you who have faith and do righteous deeds that He will surely make them successors in the earth...** (24:55) and others, and we supported it with rational proof.

The difference between the two views is that the address in the first is to the polytheists of Mecca and those who follow them specifically, while in the second, it is to the polytheists of the nation generally. The address in any case is social (اجتماعي). It is possible to combine the two views.

It is also possible that what is meant is what man witnesses in the final moments of his worldly life, where illusions fly away, claims vanish, means are nullified, and nothing remains but Allah, exalted is His Name. This is supported by the end of the verse and the subsequent verse. The pronoun in "that He is the Truth" (أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ) according to this refers to Allah, the Glorified.

There are other opinions concerning the verse which we have overlooked.

His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَكْفِ بِرَبِّكَ أَنَّهُ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ** (Is it not sufficient regarding your Lord that He is witness to all things?). The subject of "Is it not sufficient" (لَمْ يَكْفِ) is "your Lord" (بِرَبِّكَ), and the "with" (باء) is extra. "That He is witness to all things" is a substitute (بدل) for the subject. The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار). The meaning is: Is it not sufficient for the clarification of the truth that your Lord is witnessed (مشهودا) over everything? For there is nothing but that it is needy of Him from all its aspects, dependent upon Him, while He, the Exalted, is Sustainer over it and Dominant above it. Thus, He, the Exalted, is known to everything, even if some things do not recognize Him.

The connection of the sentence—namely His saying: "Is it not sufficient regarding your Lord..."—to His saying: "Soon We shall show them..." according to the last of the three aforementioned views is clear. As for the first two views, perhaps the reason is that the polytheists only disbelieved in the Quran because of its call to monotheism (التوحيد). Thus, the discourse shifted from indicating the truth of the Quran to indicating the truth of what it calls to, and then to indicating the truth of what it calls to directly without a medium. It is as if it were said: "Soon We shall show them Our signs so it becomes clear to them that the Quran which informs them of them is true, and thus it becomes clear that your Lord is One with no partner." Then it was said: "And this is a distant path; there is that which is closer than it: Is it not sufficient for them that your Lord is witnessed over everything?"

His saying, the Exalted: **أَلا إِنَّهُمْ فِي مِرْيَةٍ مِنْ لِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ** (Behold! They are indeed in doubt about the encounter with their Lord...). What the context suggests is that the verse is a notification that they do not benefit from the argumentation for His uniqueness, the Exalted, by His being a witness over everything—which is the strongest of the proofs of monotheism and the clearest for those who reason—because they are in a state of suspicion (مرية) and doubt concerning the encounter with their Lord, which is His being, the Exalted, not veiled by His attributes and acts from any of His creation.

Then He notified with His saying: **أَلا إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ مُحِيطٌ** (Behold! He indeed encompasses all things) of that by which this suspicion is removed and uprooted from its source, which is His encompassing (إحاطته), the Exalted, of everything in a manner befitting the presence of His sanctity and His greatness. Thus, no place is devoid of Him, yet He is not in a place; and nothing lacks Him, yet He is not in anything.

The commentators have views regarding this verse that, were you to review them, you would see wonders.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn 'Asakir narrated from 'Ikrimah: regarding His saying: **أَفَمَنْ يُلْقى فِي النَّارِ خَيْرٌ أَمْ مَنْ يَأْتِي آمِناً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** (Is he who is cast into the Fire better, or he who comes safe on the Day of Resurrection?), it was revealed concerning 'Ammar ibn Yasir and Abu Jahl.

I say: It was also narrated from several books from Bishr ibn Tamim. It was also narrated from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn 'Abbas: "**أَفَمَنْ يُلْقى فِي النَّارِ**" (Is he who is cast into the Fire...) he said: Abu Jahl ibn Hisham; and "**أَمْ مَنْ يَأْتِي آمِناً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ**" (or he who comes safe on the Day of Resurrection?) he said: Abu Bakr al-Siddiq. These narrations are of the nature of application (التطبيق).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالذِّكْرِ لَمَّا جاءَهُمْ** (Those who reject the Reminder when it comes to them...), meaning the Quran; **لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ** (falsehood cannot approach it from before it...), he said: "Falsehood does not come to it from the direction of the Torah, nor from the direction of the Gospel or the Psalms." **وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ** (nor from behind it), he said: "No book comes after it to invalidate it."

In *al-Majma'*, regarding the verse, it was said there are several views—until he said—and the third is that its meaning is that there is no falsehood in its reports of what has passed, nor is there falsehood in its reports of what will be in the future; rather, all its reports are in accordance with their subjects. This is what is narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon them).

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَعَرَبِيٌ** (What! A non-Arabic [Book] and an Arabic [audience]?), he said: "Had this Quran been non-Arabic, they would have said: 'How can we learn it when our tongue is Arabic and you have brought us a non-Arabic Quran?' So Allah willed to send it down in their tongue, and Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ** — **We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people.**"

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain to al-Tayyar from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **سَنُرِيهِمْ آياتِنا فِي الْآفاقِ وَفِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ** (Soon We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and in their own souls until it becomes clear to them that He is the Real), he said: "Sinking into the earth (خسف), transformation (مسخ), and pelting (قذف)." He said: I asked: "**حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ**" (until it becomes clear to them)? He said: "Leave that; that is the rising of the Upholder (القائم)."

In *al-Irshad* of al-Mufid, from 'Ali ibn Abi Hamzah from Abu al-Hasan Musa (peace be upon him): regarding the verse, he said: "The tribulations in the horizons of the earth, and the transformation (المسخ) among the enemies of the truth."

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the verse, he said: "He shows them in their own souls the transformation (المسخ), and He shows them in the horizons the collapse of the horizons upon them; thus they see the power of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, in their own souls and in the horizons." I said to him: (**حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ**)? He said: "The emergence of the Upholder (القائم) is the truth from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, which the creation shall see."

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
